does: This paper introduces MathArena as an evolving evaluation platform for mathematical reasoning in large language models, expanding beyond the original olympiad-focused benchmark to encompass diverse mathematical tasks including proof-based competitions, research-level arXiv problems, and formal proof generation in Lean. The platform implements continuous maintenance and regular benchmark updates designed to prevent saturation as model capabilities improve, while maintaining a standardized evaluation protocol across all assessed models. The work demonstrates that comprehensive, multi-faceted evaluation reveals more nuanced insights into model performance across different mathematical domains than static benchmarks alone.
connects: This work extends prior efforts to benchmark mathematical reasoning in LLMs, building directly on earlier MathArena work while addressing fundamental limitations of static benchmarking approaches. It responds to the broader pattern in machine learning where fixed benchmarks become quickly saturated and cease to differentiate model capabilities, a problem highlighted across computer vision and NLP. The expansion to include formal proof verification in Lean connects to growing work on connecting LLM reasoning to formally verifiable mathematics, while the inclusion of research-level problems bridges informal mathematical reasoning with authentic mathematical practice, extending beyond competition mathematics studied in prior olympiad-centric evaluations.
enables: This platform enables ongoing comparative analysis of mathematical reasoning as models advance, supporting more reliable claims about progress in machine learning for mathematics. By maintaining dynamic benchmarks, MathArena creates infrastructure for researchers to systematically study which mathematical capabilities emerge at different scales and how formal reasoning abilities develop. The inclusion of formal proof generation opens pathways for future work on verifiable mathematics and the integration of LLM reasoning with proof assistants like Lean, potentially enabling more trustworthy automated mathematical discovery and formalization efforts across pure and applied mathematics research domains.
